note:
西蒙·莫斯是Symphony AyasdiAI的首席执行官，Symphony AyasdiAI是一家面向金融服务的企业人工智能公司。
抱怨的是，银行似乎在采纳金融科技的聪明想法方面太慢了。他们似乎没有领会这个行业的发展方向。一些技术专家厌倦了向银行推销他们的产品，转而决定继续前进，推出自己的挑战者银行。
但老派金融家并不愚蠢。大多数人都知道“金融科技”中“买还是造”的选择是一个错误的选择。正确的问题几乎从来不是购买软件还是在内部构建软件。取而代之的是，银行经常努力走一条艰难但更聪明的道路，一直走到中间-而且这一过程正在加速。
这并不是说银行没有犯过可怕的错误。批评人士抱怨说，银行花费数十亿美元试图成为软件公司，创造了巨大的IT业务，在成本和寿命挑战上大幅裁员，并投资于无效的创新和“内部创业”努力。但总体而言，银行比试图影响它们的创业市场更了解自己的业务方式。
首先，银行拥有大多数技术专家不够用的东西：银行拥有领域专业知识。技术专家往往会对领域知识的交换价值打折扣。这是个错误。如此多的抽象技术，没有批判性的讨论，没有深入的产品管理一致性，没有清晰、清晰和商业用途，使得技术从它寻求创造的物质价值中抽象化了太多。
其次，银行并不是因为不看重企业人工智能等金融科技而舍不得买。他们之所以不情愿，是因为他们太看重它了。他们知道企业人工智能具有竞争优势，那么为什么他们要从其他所有人都依附的相同平台上获得，从相同的数据湖中提取呢？
竞争力、差异化、阿尔法、风险透明度和运营效率将取决于在令人难以置信的不久的将来如何大规模部署高生产力、高性能的认知工具。NLP、ML、AI和云的结合将在数量级上加速竞争思维。问题是，你如何拥有竞争力的关键要素？对于许多企业来说，这是一个很难回答的问题。
如果做对了，银行就可以获得其领域专业知识的真正价值，并发展出差异化的优势，而不是仅仅在某人的平台上与其他所有银行一起漂浮。他们可以定义自己行业的未来，并保持价值。人工智能是商业知识和创造力的力量倍增器。如果你不太了解你的生意，你就是在浪费钱。企业家也是如此。如果你不能让你的投资组合与业务绝对相关，你最终只能是一家咨询公司，假装是一名产品创新者。
那么，银行往好里说是谨慎，往坏里说是害怕吗？他们不想投资下一个大项目，结果却以失败告终。在金融科技的空间里，他们分不清什么是真的，什么是炒作。这是可以理解的。毕竟，他们在人工智能上花了一大笔钱。或者他们有吗？
他们似乎花了一大笔钱在所谓的人工智能内部项目上-内部项目在地狱里没有滚雪球的机会来扩展到公司的数量和并发需求。或者，他们陷入了巨大的咨询项目中，摇摇晃晃地朝着某个每个人都知道在内心深处不可能实现的崇高目标前进。
这种感觉上的不安可能对银行业有利，也可能对银行业不利，但它肯定有助于培育这家挑战者银行的新行业。
挑战者银行被广泛接受为已经转变，因为传统银行太停留在过去，无法采用他们的新想法。投资者太容易同意了。最近几周，美国挑战者银行Chime推出了一张信用卡，总部位于美国的Point推出，德国挑战者银行Vivid在金融科技旗下公司Solarisbank的帮助下推出。
传统银行也在花费资源聘请数据科学家-有时人数之多令挑战者银行家相形见绌。传统银行家希望在问题和挑战方面听取他们的数据科学家的意见，而不是花更多的钱请外部的金融科技供应商来回答或解决这些问题。
这可以说是明智之举。传统的银行家们在问自己，为什么要为他们不能100%拥有的金融科技服务付费，或者他们如何才能买到合适的部分，并保留相当于竞争优势的部分？他们不想让这种竞争优势漂浮在某个地方的数据湖里。
从银行的角度看，还是对内“金融科技”好，否则就没有竞争优势了，商业案例总是很有说服力的。问题是，银行并不是为激发设计创造力而设计的。JPMC的硬币项目是一个罕见的、非常成功的项目。然而，这是创造性的金融科技与银行之间超级结盟的一个例子，他们能够清楚地表达一个清晰、清晰的业务问题-产品需求文档，因为缺乏更好的术语。大多数内部开发都是在玩开源游戏，随着预算在投资回报方面受到严格审查，炼金术的光芒逐渐消退。
随着银行加入这些服务并收购新公司，许多人将在未来几年谈论制定新标准的问题。最终，随着银行业新选择的激增，金融科技公司和银行将联合起来，制定新的标准。
因此，花太多时间学习如何自己动手，并在其他人都在前进的时候错失良机，这是很危险的。
工程师们会告诉你，未经培训的管理层可能无法驾驭一条始终如一的路线。其结果是，随着发展水平的要求不断曲折前进，技术债务不断积累。给你的数据科学家和工程师施加太大的压力也会导致技术债务更快地堆积起来。错误或低效被留在原地。新功能是作为变通办法构建的。
这就是为什么内部构建的软件有不能伸缩的名声的原因之一。同样的问题也出现在顾问开发的软件中。系统中的旧问题隐藏在新问题的下面，裂缝开始在建立在低质量代码之上的新应用程序中显现出来。
这是一个有点沉闷的答案，但成功来自谦逊。它需要理解，大问题是通过创造性的团队来解决的，每个人都知道他们带来了什么，每个人都得到了平等的尊重，并在需要解决什么和什么是成功的问题上完全清晰地进行了管理。
再加上一些斯大林式的项目管理，你成功的可能性就会上升一个数量级。因此，未来的成功将看到银行拥有更少但更值得信任的金融科技合作伙伴，他们共同珍视自己创造的知识产权。他们必须尊重这一点，没有对方，任何一方都不能成功。这是一个很难破解的密码。但如果没有它，银行就会陷入困境，寻求与它们合作的企业家也是如此。
note_en:
Simon Moss is CEO of  Symphony AyasdiAI, an enterprise artificial intelligence company for financial services.
  The complaint is banks are seemingly too slow to adopt fintech’s bright ideas. They don’t seem to grasp where the industry is headed. Some technologists, tired of marketing their wares to banks, have instead decided to go ahead and launch their own challenger banks.
 But old-school financiers aren’t dumb. Most know the “buy versus build” choice in fintech is a false choice. The right question is almost never whether to buy software or build it internally. Instead, banks have often worked to walk the difficult but smarter path right down the middle — and that’s accelerating.
   That’s not to say banks haven’t made horrendous mistakes. Critics complain about banks spending billions trying to be software companies, creating huge IT businesses with huge redundancies in cost and longevity challenges, and investing into ineffectual innovation and “intrapreneurial” endeavors. But overall, banks know their business way better than the entrepreneurial markets that seek to influence them.
 First, banks have something most technologists don’t have enough of: Banks have domain expertise. Technologists tend to discount the exchange value of domain knowledge. And that’s a mistake. So much abstract technology, without critical discussion, deep product management alignment and crisp, clear and business-usefulness, makes too much technology abstract from the material value it seeks to create.
 Second, banks are not reluctant to buy because they don’t value enterprise artificial intelligence and other fintech. They’re reluctant because they value it too much. They know enterprise AI gives a competitive edge, so why should they get it from the same platform everyone else is attached to, drawing from the same data lake?
 Competitiveness, differentiation, alpha, risk transparency and operational productivity will be defined by how highly productive, high-performance cognitive tools are deployed at scale in the incredibly near future. The combination of NLP, ML, AI and cloud will accelerate competitive ideation in order of magnitude. The question is, how do you own the key elements of competitiveness? It’s a tough question for many enterprises to answer.
 If they get it right, banks can obtain the true value of their domain expertise and develop a differentiated edge where they don’t just float along with every other bank on someone’s platform. They can define the future of their industry and keep the value. AI is a force multiplier for business knowledge and creativity. If you don’t know your business well, you’re wasting your money. Same goes for the entrepreneur. If you can’t make your portfolio absolutely business relevant, you end up being a consulting business pretending to be a product innovator.
  So are banks at best cautious, and at worst afraid? They don’t want to invest in the next big thing only to have it flop. They can’t distinguish what’s real from hype in the fintech space. And that’s understandable. After all, they have spent a fortune on AI. Or have they?
 It seems they have spent a fortune on stuff called AI — internal projects with not a snowball’s chance in hell to scale to the volume and concurrency demands of the firm. Or they have become enmeshed in huge consulting projects staggering toward some lofty objective that everyone knows deep down is not possible.
 This perceived trepidation may or may not be good for banking, but it certainly has helped foster the new industry of the challenger bank.
 Challenger banks are widely accepted to have come around because traditional banks are too stuck in the past to adopt their new ideas. Investors too easily agree. In recent weeks, American challenger banks  Chime unveiled a credit card, U.S.-based  Point launched and German challenger bank  Vivid launched with the help of Solarisbank, a fintech company.
  Traditional banks are spending resources on hiring data scientists too — sometimes in numbers that dwarf the challenger bankers. Legacy bankers want to listen to their data scientists on questions and challenges rather than pay more for an external fintech vendor to answer or solve them.
 This arguably is the smart play. Traditional bankers are asking themselves why should they pay for fintech services that they can’t 100% own, or how can they buy the right bits, and retain the parts that amount to a competitive edge? They don’t want that competitive edge floating around in a data lake somewhere.
 From banks’ perspective, it’s better to “fintech” internally or else there’s no competitive advantage; the business case is always compelling. The problem is a bank is not designed to stimulate creativity in design. JPMC’s COIN project is a rare and fantastically successful project. Though, this is an example of a super alignment between creative fintech and the bank being able to articulate a clear, crisp business problem — a Product Requirements Document for want of a better term. Most internal development is playing games with open source, with the shine of the alchemy wearing off as budgets are looked at hard in respect to return on investment.
 A lot of people are going to talk about setting new standards in the coming years as banks onboard these services and buy new companies. Ultimately, fintech firms and banks are going to join together and make the new standard as new options in banking proliferate.
  So, there’s a danger to spending too much time learning how to do it yourself and missing the boat as everyone else moves ahead.
 Engineers will tell you that untutored management can fail to steer a consistent course. The result is an accumulation of technical debt as development-level requirements keep zigzagging. Laying too much pressure on your data scientists and engineers can also lead to technical debt piling up faster. A bug or an inefficiency is left in place. New features are built as workarounds.
 This is one reason why in-house-built software has a reputation for not scaling. The same problem shows up in consultant-developed software. Old problems in the system hide underneath new ones and the cracks begin to show in the new applications built on top of low-quality code.
  It’s a bit of a dull answer, but success comes from humility. It needs an understanding that big problems are solved with creative teams, each understanding what they bring, each being respected as equals and managed in a completely clear articulation on what needs to be solved and what success looks like.
 Throw in some Stalinist project management and your probability of success goes up an order of magnitude. So, the successes of the future will see banks having fewer but way more trusted fintech partners that jointly value the intellectual property they are creating. They’ll have to respect that neither can succeed without the other. It’s a tough code to crack. But without it, banks are in trouble, and so are the entrepreneurs that seek to work with them.
title: 银行并不像企业人工智能和金融科技企业家认为的那样愚蠢
title_en: Banks aren’t as stupid as enterprise AI and fintech entrepreneurs think

note:
埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)将发布Neuralink的最新进展，Neuralink是他创立的公司和技术，旨在在我们的大脑和计算机之间建立直接的、超低延迟的连接。更新将在太平洋时间下午3点(美国东部时间下午6点)开始，并将在上面进行现场直播。
根据马斯克的推文，我们应该看到的是Neuralink设备的实际产品演示。这位兼任首席执行官的人表示，这将是Neuralink去年在一次类似的更新中透露的机器人的第二版。该机器人是一个外科自动化平台，旨在执行高精度的脑外科手术，该手术植入Neuralink技术的内部部分，最终将与头骨外部的接收器进行无线通信，然后接收器可以将基于思维的输入传输到计算机-如果开发达到其崇高目标的话。
马斯克在某种程度上降低了人们的期望--我们将看到的仍然很大程度上是一种“仅供患有极端医疗问题的患者使用的实验性医疗设备”，而不是他希望有朝一日实现的为普通消费者用户设计的界面的最终愿景。但人们的期望仍然很高，因为该公司去年已经开始进行动物试验，并正在讨论在未来12个月内可能进入人体试验。
note_en:
Elon Musk   is set to deliver a progress update for Neuralink, the company and technology he founded that aims to create a direct, ultra-low latency connection between our brains and our computers. The update will kick off at 3 PM PT (6 PM ET), and will be streamed live above.
 Based on Musk’s tweets, what we should see is an actual product demo of a  Neuralink   device in action. The multi-CEO has said that it will be a  version 2 of the robot that Neuralink revealed last year during a similar update. That robot is a surgical automated platform that’s designed to perform the highly precise brain surgery that implants the internal part of Neuralink’s tech, which will ultimately communicate wireless with a receiver on the outside of the skull that can then transmit thought-based input to computers – if development reaches its lofty goals.
  Musk has tempered expectations somewhat – what we’ll see is still very much an “experimental medical device for use only in patients with extreme medical problems,” and not the ultimate vision of an interface designed for general consumer user that he hopes to someday achieve. But expectations are still high, given that last year, the company had embarked on animal testing, and was talking about potentially entering human testing within the next 12 months.
title: 观看埃隆·马斯克的Neuralink脑机接口进展更新实况
title_en: Watch Elon Musk’s Neuralink brain computer interface progress update live

note:
本周早些时候，第一幕风险投资公司(Act One Ventures)推出了一项新的多样性和包容性倡议，名为多样性条款单骑手，在上限表上担任代表。多元化骑手的目的是通过让黑人和其他代表性不足的投资者成为共同投资者，增加他们在交易中的数量。
格雷克罗夫特合伙公司(Greycroft Partners)、第一轮资本(First Rround Capital)、Maveron、Five Wall、Plexo Capital和Premior Ventures等公司已经承诺了这一点。这意味着公司将在其标准条款单中包括样板语言：
为了推动风险投资行业的多元化努力，本公司和主要投资者[基金名称]将尽商业最大努力提供并尽一切努力将至少一个黑人[或其他代表不足的群体，包括但不限于Latinx、女性、LGBTQ+]支票出票人(DCW)作为共同投资者参与融资，并为此类共同投资者分配最少[X]%或[X]美元的总融资。
这当然是在为黑人、拉丁裔和原住民以及科技行业其他代表性不足群体的人们创造额外财富机会的道路上迈出的很好的一步。然而，更有力的步骤将是去掉“尽最大努力”和“尽一切努力”的部分，因为正如它目前所写的那样，承诺是基于相当主观的条件进行对冲的。相反，以下内容会更好：
为了推动风险投资行业的多元化努力，本公司和主要投资者[基金名称]将尽商业最大努力提供并尽一切努力将至少一个黑人[或其他代表不足的群体，包括但不限于Latinx、女性、LGBTQ+]支票出票人(DCW)作为共同投资者参与融资，并为此类共同投资者分配最少[X]%或[X]美元的总融资。
在All Raise为黑人女性创始人举办的第二届年度活动上，创始人梅特·芬德(Met Funder)、Planet FWD首席执行官朱莉娅·柯林斯(Julia Collins)采访了YC首席执行官迈克尔·塞贝尔(Michael Seibel)，谈到了他在科技方面的经验和给创始人的。
他告诉听众：“当我开始创业的时候，那是2006年，没有多少人看起来像我们中的任何一个人在做创业。”“我想你会想到的是公然的歧视，但实际上我得到的是其他东西，没有反馈。”
他接着说，人们不敢批评他，因为担心被人以某种方式看待。
这就是西贝尔说他已经成为那种会告诉创始人每个人的想法的人的部分原因。
Seibel说：“不管你同意与否，我希望你能有一个很好的心理模型，知道人们在想什么，不会说什么。”
本周，Insta购物者通过零工集体再次发出了自己的声音。鉴于野火和其他预期的与气候变化相关的灾难，Insta的员工希望公司为Insta的运营关闭的每一天提供相当于前30天平均每日工资(包括小费)的每日灾难赔偿金。此外，GWC希望Insta关闭其在城市已宣布进入紧急状态或发布疏散命令的市场的运营。
在提出这些要求之前不久，Insta同意向旧金山的一些Insta工人分配727,985美元，作为与医疗保健和带薪病假福利有关的和解协议的一部分。
与此同时，Insta还面临着华盛顿总检察长就其“欺骗性”服务费提起的新诉讼。诉讼要求支付这些服务费的消费者赔偿，以及拖欠华盛顿特区的税款和利息。
今年早些时候，我们开始探索合作创业的案例，在这种情况下，工人和用户有机会获得公司的真正所有权和控制权，产生的任何利润都会返还给成员或再投资于公司。
今天许多科技公司的创建方式并不一定是这样的。Start.coop是一家面向合作社的技术加速器，它正试图帮助建设这一新的未来。本周，Start.coop获得了15万美元的承诺，每年帮助资助两类新的初创公司。Start.coop向每个初创公司投资1.5万美元，所有毕业生都成为Start.coop的股东，Start.coop本身是一个合作社，通过加速器在工人、投资者、顾问和初创公司之间分配所有权。
技术几乎颠覆了经济的每一个部分。它颠覆了零工经济、游戏、购物和预订酒店的方式。但有一件事是科技行业一直不愿触及的，那就是所有权本身。也就是说，谁变得富有，谁从这些公司的成长中受益。这一点真的没有改变。在某些方面，科技行业只是在经济的其他各个领域重现财富不平等。
与此同时，退出社区运动的幕后黑手们正准备发布一本杂志，概述初创公司通向IPO和收购以外的结果的途径。E2C是一个正在进行的项目，旨在探索如何帮助初创企业将投资者拥有的所有权转变为社区所有权，社区所有权可能包括用户、客户、工人或所有利益相关者的某种组合。
Zebras Unite联合创始人兼“Zine”杂志联合作者玛拉·泽佩达告诉TechCrunch：“这本杂志的目的是为对话中需要发生的所有方面提供一个初步的路线图，这样我们就可以省去创始人在认识和验证他们不合适的时候的痛苦，我们需要共同创造适合他们的东西。”“这不是灵丹妙药。又不是每个人都需要做另一件完美的事。我将其描述为进行寒武纪的一系列实验，以便弄清楚未来是什么。这不仅仅是一件事。这就是我们正在做的事情是如何真正不同的。有时会有这些利基产品或运动跳出来说，“这就是答案。目前还没有一个答案。“。
下周要关注更深层次的研究。现在，这里有一些关于这个主题的额外阅读。
note_en:
Earlier this week,   Act One Ventures launched a new diversity and inclusion initiative called The Diversity Term Sheet Rider for Representation at the Cap Table . The purpose of the Diversity Rider is to increase the number of Black and other underrepresented investors in deals by making them co-investors.
 Already, firms like Greycroft Partners, First Round Capital, Maveron, Fifth Wall, Plexo Capital and Precursor Ventures have committed to it. What that means is firms will include boilerplate language in their standard term sheets:
 In order to advance diversity efforts in the venture capital industry, the Company and the lead investor, [Fund Name], will make commercial best efforts to offer and make every attempt to include as a co-investor in the financing at least one Black [or other underrepresented group including, but not limited to LatinX, women, LGBTQ+] check writer (DCWs), and to allocate a minimum of [X]% or [X] $’s of the total round for such co-investor.
 This is certainly a good step on the road to creating additional wealth opportunities for Black, Latinx and Indigenous people, as well folks from other underrepresented groups in tech. However, a stronger step would be to remove the parts about “best efforts” and “make every attempt” because, as it’s currently written, the commitment hedges on rather subjective conditions. Instead, the following would be better:
 In order to advance diversity efforts in the venture capital industry, the Company and the lead investor, [Fund Name], will   make commercial best efforts to offer and make every attempt to  include as a co-investor in the financing at least one Black [or other underrepresented group including, but not limited to LatinX, women, LGBTQ+] check writer (DCWs), and to allocate a minimum of [X]% or [X] $’s of the total round for such co-investor.
  At All Raise’s second annual event for Black female founders, When Founder Met Funder, Planet FWD CEO Julia Collins interviewed YC CEO Michael Seibel about his experience in tech and tips for founders.
 “When I started doing startups, it was 2006 and there weren’t many people who looked like any of us that were doing startups,” he told the audience. “I think what you would’ve expected was overt discrimination but actually I got something else, which was no feedback.”
 He went on to say that people were afraid to be critical of him, for fear of being perceived a certain way.
  That’s partly why Seibel says he’s become the type of person who will tell founders what everyone is thinking.
 “Agree with it or disagree with it, I want you to have a good mental model of what people are thinking and not saying,” Seibel said.
     Instacart shoppers, via Gig Workers Collective,  made their voices heard again this week. In light of the wildfires and other anticipated climate change-related disasters, Instacart workers want the company to provide disaster pay at a daily rate equal to the average rate of daily pay, including tips, over the previous 30 days for each day Instacart’s operations are shutdown. Additionally, GWC wants Instacart to shut down its operations in markets where a city has declared a state of emergency or issued evacuations.
 The demands came shortly after   Instacart agreed to distribute $727,985  among some San Francisco-based Instacart workers as part of a settlement pertaining to health care and paid sick leave benefits.
 Meanwhile,  Instacart is also facing a new lawsuit from DC’s attorney general over its “deceptive” service fees. The suit seeks restitution for consumers who paid those service fees, as well as back taxes and interest on taxes owed to D.C.
     We began exploring earlier in the year the   case for cooperative startups , where workers and users have the opportunity to gain true ownership and control in a company, and where any profits that are generated are returned to the members or reinvested in the company.
 The way many tech companies are built today don’t need to be that way. Start.coop, a tech accelerator for cooperatives, is trying to help build this new future.  This week, Start.coop, received a $150,000 commitment to help fund two new classes of startups per year. Start.coop invests $15,000 in each startup and all graduates become shareholders in Start.coop, which is a cooperative itself that distributes ownership among workers, investors, advisors and startups that go through the accelerator.
  Technology has disrupted almost every part of the economy. It’s disrupted the gig economy, gaming, shopping and how to book hotels. But the one thing that the technology sector has not been willing to touch is ownership itself. That is, who gets rich and who benefits from the growth of these companies. That really hasn’t changed. In some ways, the tech sector is just recreating the wealth inequality in every other part of the economy.
  Meanwhile, the folks behind the Exit to Community movement are gearing up to   release a zine  outlining startup paths to outcomes other than IPOs and acquisitions. E2C is a working project that explores ways to help startups transition investor-owned to community ownership, which could include users, customers, workers or some combination of all stakeholders.
 “The purpose of the zine is to provide an initial roadmap to all of the aspects of the conversation that need to happen so we can save founders pain in recognizing and validating they’re in the wrong fit and we need to co-create what does fit,” Zebras Unite co-founder and zine co-author Mara Zepeda told TechCrunch. “It’s not a silver bullet. It’s not like there’s this other perfect thing that everyone needs to do. I describe it as running a Cambrian explosion of experiments in order to figure out what this future is. It’s not just one thing. That’s how what we’re doing is really different. Sometimes there are these niche products or movements that pop up and say, “this is the answer. There isn’t one answer for this moment.”
 Be on the lookout for a deeper-dive into this next week. For now,   here’s some additional reading on the topic .
title: 人力资本：“人们害怕对我吹毛求疵”
title_en: Human Capital: ‘People were afraid of being critical with me’

note:
自从智能手机以其无限的可能性和多巴胺的点击吸引了我们，市长和城市官僚们对智能洗涤他们的城市的概念就永远也听不够了。这让他们听起来充满活力，对企业也很有吸引力。神童简化了你运行服务的责任，优化了效率，并让市民安全地使用了一堆有趣的应用程序，这有什么不好的呢？
智能城市没有具体的定义，但高科技版本承诺使用摄像头和传感器来监控每个人和一切，从垃圾桶到桥梁，并使用产生的数据来帮助城市平稳运行。谷歌的姊妹公司Sideway Labs提出的一项高调提议，要对多伦多12英亩的土地进行智能改造，但这一提议正面临巨大的反弹。9月份，一份独立报告称这些计划“抽象得令人沮丧”；反过来，美国科技投资者罗杰·麦克纳米(Roger McNamee)警告称，谷歌不能信任这些数据，称该项目是“监视资本主义”。
正如多伦多大学的肖珊娜·萨克斯(Shoshanna Saxe)所强调的那样，还有一些实际的考虑。她在7月份的“纽约时报”(New York Times)上写道，智能城市“将非常难以管理，存在各种不可预测的脆弱性”。科技产品老化速度很快：当传感器发生故障时会发生什么？城市能够负担得起昂贵的新技术人员团队，以及留住他们仍然需要的地面工人吗？“如果智能数据识别出一条需要铺设的道路，”她写道，“它仍然需要人们带着沥青和压路机出现。”
萨克斯精辟地呼吁将我们的部分精力转向建设“优秀的哑巴城市”。她并不是反对技术，只是她认为智慧城市可能是不必要的。她说：“对于我们的许多挑战，我们不需要新技术或新想法；我们需要意志、远见和勇气来利用最好的旧想法。”
萨克斯是对的。事实上，她可以走得更远。有旧的，然后是旧的-对于越来越容易受到洪水、不利天气、碳超载、令人窒息的污染以及人与自然之间不健康的脱节的城市景观来说，有一个强有力的理由将目光从旧技术转向古老技术。
在这种智慧永远消失之前，将古老的如何与自然共生的知识编织到我们如何塑造未来的城市中，是非常有可能的。我们可以重新开发我们的城市景观，并将低技术的生态解决方案应用于排水、废水处理、洪水生存、当地农业和污染，这些解决方案已经为土著人民工作了数千年，不需要电子传感器、计算机服务器或额外的IT支持。
本月，哈佛大学(Harvard University)和哥伦比亚大学(Columbia University)的城市设计讲师朱莉娅·沃森(Julia Watson)与出版商塔森(Taschen)共同出版了她的书“Lo-Tek：由激进的原住民主义设计”。这是20多年旅行的结果，通过建筑师的视角来研究原始的智能住区。
她参观了伊拉克的马丹人(Ma‘dan People)，他们用芦苇编织建筑物和漂浮的岛屿；新墨西哥州的祖尼人(Zuni)，他们建造“华夫饼花园”，为沙漠作物耕种收集、储存和操纵水；以及巴厘岛的Subak水稻梯田。沃森走在活的树根桥上，这些桥比任何人造结构都能更好地抵御恶劣天气，并且允许印度北部的卡西山区部落在季风洪水期间在村庄之间旅行。
沃森说：“有这么多不同的方法可以让城市恢复原状。”这不仅仅是在城市中重塑一个古老的系统，而是让复杂的生态系统适应不同类型的地方，它们有自己独特的要求。以她目前正在为位于香港珠江口的高层城市深圳工作的一个提案为例。它曾经是一个渔村，然后是一个纺织小镇，“然后它就一飞冲天了，”沃森说。“所有吸收三角洲地貌中所有水分的鱼塘、围塘、堤坝和湿地都正在被抹去。因此，这座城市的发展方式正在抹去当地景观中的弹性。“。
但她说，你不一定要擦除才能前进。“你可以跨越和嵌入当地的智慧，使用一种基于自然的中国传统技术，这种技术具有气候弹性、生态弹性和文化弹性。我们也可以用它们来创造美丽的城市空间。“。
1964年和2015年从香港顶山俯瞰罗湖口岸和深圳。图片来源：1964年图片由大卫·麦克劳德提供，2015年由Lucien Long为《卫报》提供。
北京大学设计学教授于孔健(音译)同意这一理念。被称为“海绵城市”建筑师的俞敏洪在中国创造了被动吸收雨水的城市景观，使用在季风期间泛滥的渗透性人行道、绿色屋顶和梯田湿地公园。如果湿地位于建筑物的上游，它们将在洪水到达市区之前泛滥。
这些公园把鱼和鸟带回了城市，俞敏洪说，“人们喜欢它。”他说，这些项目“表现良好，其中许多都经过了超过10年的测试，当然可以在世界其他地区复制。”事实上，具有讽刺意味的是，本月他访问了孟加拉国，“帮助他们的‘智慧城市’项目”，在那里，他说服了“负责自然的部长，让他们相信自然是聪明的，我们古老的智慧告诉我们如何以聪明的方式与自然相处。”
哥本哈根也选择了一个愚蠢的解决方案，也就是当地规划者所说的“绿色和蓝色”，来解决日益增加的洪水风险：即一系列公园在暴风雨中可以变成湖泊。该市估计，它们的成本将比修建堤坝和新下水道的成本低三分之一，而且还会带来重新野生的额外生态效益。2010年，一个废弃的军事地点被清理干净，重新荒野成为一个自然保护区，经常供放牧动物使用，名为阿马格尔自然中心(Amager Nature Centre)-这是一个巨大的公园，不仅有快乐的人们漫步和骑自行车，还有昆虫、受保护的两栖动物、稀有鸟类和鹿。
但是，愚蠢的城市甚至可以比这更聪明。功能性湿地不仅可以保护城市免受洪水侵袭，恢复自然，还可以净化废水。它们比污水处理厂效率更高，同时吸收了大量的碳、氮、硫和甲烷，并创造了渔业和肥沃的农田。不需要水、能量、处理化学品或鱼饲料。世界上最大的此类系统位于印度加尔各答东部，涉及城市污水喂养鱼类。它每年为该市节省约2200万美元(1700万英镑)的垃圾处理厂运营成本。这些水可以用于灌溉，进一步节省了50万GB的水和化肥成本。而且它使城市的大部分食物都可以在当地种植。
或者，随着全球水位上升，我们可以向Makoko学习，Makoko是拉各斯令人难以置信的高跷城市，居住着8万居民。它的“漂浮学校”--可持续的、以太阳能为燃料的--吸引了世界的想象力。鹿特丹已经引入了漂浮森林和农场，并正在制定可持续漂浮城市的计划。
至于愚蠢的交通方式，毫无疑问，步行或骑自行车比开车出行要好于城市短途旅行：零污染，零碳排放，自由锻炼。
对于空调的普及，有一个愚蠢的解决方案，空调是城市中最耗能的产品之一：更多的植物。威斯康星州麦迪逊的一项研究发现，如果树木覆盖率达到40%，城市温度可以降低5%。植被密度高的绿色屋顶可以为建筑物降温高达60%。或者你可以像虫子一样思考：建筑师正在模仿白蚁洞穴的自然冷却气流。米克·皮尔斯(Mick Pearce)位于津巴布韦首都哈拉雷的35万平方英尺的伊斯特盖特中心(Eastgate Centre)于20世纪90年代竣工，至今仍被誉为哑巴空调的典范：它所需要的只是风扇，消耗的能源是隔壁建筑的十分之一。
几堵象征性的绿墙和几棵树做不到这一点。沃森呼吁关注永久养殖：自给自足的生态系统。“如果它是一片城市森林，”她说，“也许它在城市的中心，也可能在外围，或者它可能是一个内部环境-一个设计成拥有复杂生态系统的中庭，同时也具有农业生产力。”
有数百种基于自然的技术从未被探索过。例如，沃森设想了卡西山区部落活着的根桥在城市中令人惊叹的用途：“可以种植它们来减少城市热岛效应，方法是增加街道沿线的树冠覆盖率，将树根训练成桁架，与街道的建筑相结合-本质上，消除树木和建筑之间的区别。”它们甚至可以在季节性洪水期间保留原来的用途-水上的活的、物理的桥梁。
今年4月，格里塔·通伯格(Greta Thunberg)和“卫报”(Guardian)专栏作家乔治·蒙比特(George Monbiot)制作了一段集会视频，呼吁增加树木和湿地，并覆盖植物，以应对气候危机。城市可以成为这一努力的一部分。
智能城市的想法诞生于沃森所说的“同样的人类优越感--认为自然应该得到控制的情结”。缺少的是共生。“地球上的生命是建立在共生的基础上的，”沃森说。她建议我们用“最共生的生存”来代替“适者生存”。也许没有那么朗朗上口。但是更聪明。

note_en:
Ever since smartphones hooked us with their limitless possibilities and dopamine hits, mayors and city bureaucrats can’t get enough of the notion of smart-washing their cities. It makes them sound dynamic and attractive to business. What’s not to love about whizzkids streamlining your responsibilities for running services, optimising efficiency and keeping citizens safe into a bunch of fun apps?
 There’s no concrete definition of a smart city, but high-tech versions promise to use cameras and sensors to monitor everyone and everything, from bins to bridges, and use the resulting data to help the city run smoothly. One high-profile proposal by Google’s sister company, Sidewalk Labs, to give 12 acres of Toronto a smart makeover is facing a massive backlash. In September, an independent report called the plans  “frustratingly abstract”; in turn US tech investor Roger McNamee warned Google can’t be trusted with such data, calling the project “surveillance capitalism”.
  There are practical considerations, too, as Shoshanna Saxe of the University of Toronto has highlighted.  Smart cities, she wrote in the New York Times in July, “will be exceedingly complex to manage, with all sorts of unpredictable vulnerabilities”. Tech products age fast: what happens when the sensors fail? And can cities afford expensive new teams of tech staff, as well as keeping the ground workers they’ll still need? “If smart data identifies a road that needs paving,” she writes, “it still needs people to show up with asphalt and a steamroller.”
 Saxe pithily calls for redirecting some of our energy toward building “excellent dumb cities.” She’s not anti-technology, it’s just that she thinks smart cities may be unnecessary. “For many of our challenges, we don’t need new technologies or new ideas; we need the will, foresight and courage to use the best of the old ideas,” she says.
  Saxe is right. In fact, she could go further. There’s old, and then there’s  old – and for urban landscapes increasingly vulnerable to floods, adverse weather, carbon overload, choking pollution and an unhealthy disconnect between humans and nature, there’s a strong case for looking beyond old technologies to ancient technologies.
 It is eminently possible to weave ancient knowledge of how to live symbiotically with nature into how we shape the cities of the future, before this wisdom is lost forever. We can rewild our urban landscapes, and apply low-tech ecological solutions to drainage, wastewater processing, flood survival, local agriculture and pollution that have worked for indigenous peoples for thousands of years, with no need for electronic sensors, computer servers or extra IT support.
 This month, Julia Watson, a lecturer in urban design at Harvard and Columbia Universities, launched her book  Lo-Tek: Design by Radical Indigenism, with publisher Taschen. It’s the result of more than 20 years of travelling to research the original smart settlements, through an architect’s lens.
  She visited the Ma’dan people in Iraq, who weave buildings and floating islands from reeds; the Zuni people in New Mexico, who create “waffle gardens” to capture, store and manipulate water for desert crop farming; and the subak rice terraces of Bali. Watson walked the living tree-root bridges that can withstand adverse weather better than any human-made structure, and that allow the Khasi hill tribe in Northern India to travel between villages during the monsoon floods.
  “There are so many different ways you can rewild cities,” says Watson – and it’s not just a case of plonking an ancient system in a city, but rather adapting complex ecosystems for different types of places with their own unique requirements. Take a current proposal she is working on for the high-rise city of Shenzhen on the Pearl River estuary by Hong Kong. It was once a fishing village, then a textile town, “and it just skyrocketed,” says Watson. “All of the fishponds and polders and dykes and wetlands that absorb all the water in that delta landscape are being erased. So the city is developing in a way that’s erasing the indigenous resilience in the landscape.”
 But you don’t have to erase to go forwards, she says. “You can leapfrog and embed local intelligence, using a nature-based traditional Chinese technology that’s climate resilient, ecologically resilient and culturally resilient. And we can make beautiful urban spaces with them as well.”
 View of Lowu border crossing and Shenzhen from Crest Hill, Hong Kong in 1964 and 2015. Credits: 1964 image courtesy of David McLeod and 2015 by Lucien Long for the Guardian
 Kongjian Yu, a design professor at Peking University, agrees with this philosophy. Known as  the “sponge cities” architect, Yu creates urban landscapes in China that  passively absorb rainwater, using permeable pavements, green roofs and terraced wetland parks that flood during monsoon. If wetlands are situated upriver of the buildings, they will flood before the water reaches the city proper.
  The parks have brought fish and birds back to cities, says Yu, “and people love it.” The projects, he says, “are performing well, and many of them have been tested for over 10 years, and can certainly be replicable in other parts of the world.” In fact, this month he has visited Bangladesh, ironically, “helping their ‘smart city’ project,” where he has convinced “the minister in charge that nature is smart, and our ancient wisdom tells us how to live with nature in a smart way.”
 Copenhagen, too, has opted for a dumb – or, as local planners call it, “a green and blue” – solution to their increasing flood risks: namely, a series of parks that can become lakes during storms. The city estimated they would cost a third less than building levees and new sewers, and come with the added ecological benefits of rewilding. An abandoned military site was cleaned up in 2010 and rewilded into a nature reserve and common for grazing animals, the Amager Nature Centre – a vast park with not only happy people meandering and cycling around but insects, protected amphibians, rare birds and deer.
  But dumb cities can be even smarter than that. Not only can functioning wetlands defend cities against floods and restore nature, they can clean wastewater. And they can do it more efficiently than sewage-treatment works – all while absorbing a whole lot of carbon, nitrogen, sulphur and methane, and creating a fishing industry and fertile farmland. No water, energy, treatment chemicals or fish feed required. The world’s largest such system, in  east Kolkata in India, involves the city’s sewage feeding the fish. It saves the city approximately  $22m (£17m) a year in running costs for a waste treatment plant. The water can be used for irrigation, saving a further £500,000 in water and fertiliser costs. And it enables much of the city’s food to be grown locally.
 Or, as waters rise globally, we can learn from  Makoko, the incredible city-on-stilts in Lagos that is home to 80,000 residents. Its  “floating school” – sustainable and solar-fuelled – has captured the world’s imagination. Rotterdam has already introduced a floating forest and farm, and is developing plans for a sustainable floating city.
  As for dumb transport, there can be no doubt that walking or cycling are superior to car travel over short urban distances: zero pollution, zero carbon emissions, free exercise.
 And there’s a dumb solution to the spread of air conditioning, one of the greatest urban energy guzzlers: more plants. A study in  Madison, Wisconsin found that urban temperatures can be 5% cooler with 40% tree cover. Green roofs with high vegetation density  can cool buildings by up to 60%. Or you could just think like a bug: architects are mimicking the natural cooling airflows of termite burrows. Mick Pearce’s 350,000 sq ft  Eastgate Centre in Zimbabwe’s capital, Harare, completed in the 1990s, is still held up as a paragon of dumb air conditioning: all it needs are fans, and uses a tenth of the energy of the buildings next door.
 A few token green walls and trees won’t do it. Watson calls for a focus on permaculture: self-sustaining ecosystems. “If it’s an urban forest,” she says, “perhaps it’s in the centre of the city, perhaps it’s on the periphery, or it could be an interior environment – an atrium designed to have a complex ecosystem that’s also agriculturally productive.”
  There are hundreds of nature-based technologies that have never been explored. For example, Watson envisions stunning urban uses for the living root bridges of the Khasi hill tribe: “They could be grown to reduce the urban heat island effect by increasing canopy cover along streets, with roots trained into trusses that integrate with the architecture of the street – in essence, removing the distinction between tree and building.” They could even retain their original use during seasonal floods – living, physical bridges over the water.
 In April, Greta Thunberg and Guardian columnist George Monbiot  made a rallying video calling for more trees and wetlands and plant cover to tackle the climate crisis. Cities can be part of this push.
 The idea of smart cities is born of what Watson describes as “the same human superiority-complex that thinks nature should be controlled”. What’s missing is symbiosis. “Life on Earth is based upon symbiosis,” Watson says. She suggests we replace the saying “survival of the fittest” with “survival of the most symbiotic”. Not as catchy, perhaps. But smarter.
 Follow Guardian Cities on  Twitter,  Facebook and  Instagram to join the discussion and catch up on  our best stories
title: 让低技术城市变得“哑巴”而不是“智能”的理由
title_en: The case for making low-tech 'dumb' cities instead of 'smart' ones

note:
内华达州里诺市一名25岁的居民两次感染大流行冠状病毒SARS-CoV-2，相隔约48天，第二次感染导致新冠肺炎的病情比第一次更严重，需要住院和氧气支持。
这是根据由内华达大学的研究人员领导并发布在网上的一项研究草案得出的。这项研究没有在科学期刊上发表，也没有经过同行审查。尽管如此，它还是很快引起了研究人员的注意，他们一直在检查本周早些时候报道的第一例SARS-CoV-2再次感染确诊病例的数据。
再次感染SARS-CoV-2并不令人惊讶，甚至不一定令人担忧。从一个人到另一个人，对感染的免疫反应是沿着谱系发展的，一些人增强了强大的保护性反应，而另一些人的反应较弱。在全球2450多万病例中，完全预计会发现一些康复的患者，他们的免疫反应没有完全保护，因此很容易再次感染。
最大的问题是：这种情况有多常见？研究人员仍然不知道。这个病例可能很好地代表了一个非常罕见的事件，但有许多关于SARS-CoV-2免疫反应的重大问题，研究人员正在争先恐后地了解。为什么有些人比其他人患上更严重的疾病？对于那些康复的人，什么水平的免疫反应将保护他们免受另一次感染？有多少康复人员会产生这些保护性反应？这些保护性反应能持续多久？
虽然新的再感染报告是一个重要的数据点，但它-就像之前的再感染报告一样-没有回答这些大问题中的任何一个。
从这些报告中我们能说的是，再感染是可能的--至少对某些人来说是可能的。再说一次，这并不令人惊讶，但要证明这一点却很棘手。就像本周早些时候的报告一样，内华达州的研究人员转向全基因组测序，以证明他们的患者确实有两种截然不同的感染。
具体地说，研究人员对从第一次和第二次疾病期间采集的鼻拭子中收集的全部SARS-CoV-2遗传物质进行了解码。研究人员发现，在这两种疾病期间，患者鼻子中存在的病毒在代码上有许多细微的差异。第一种病毒从第二种病毒中缺失了四种代码变体，第二种病毒从第一种病毒中缺失了七种密码变体。虽然微小的基因变化往往会随着时间的推移在病毒中自然积累，但研究人员计算出，如果这两种疾病实际上是由同一种病毒引起的，那么这一名患者的病毒进化速度将比之前记录的速度快3.6倍以上。此外，作者说，在两种病毒之间看到的变化中，有四种只能用基因“逆转”来解释，而发生这种变化的可能性“微乎其微”。
尽管如此，除了遗传证据表明有两种不同的感染外，总有一个问题是，是否与样本发生了一些混淆。也就是说，也许病人没有从鼻子里取出不同的病毒株；也许拭子只是贴错了标签，或者在实验室里处理不当。为了避免这种担忧，研究人员对棉签储存的液体和中间样品制备进行了基因法医测试。研究人员发现，所有与鼻拭子样本相关的人类衍生材料都来自同一个人。
虽然研究人员提出了一个有说服力的案例，证明他们的患者实际上感染了两次，但关于患者免疫反应的关键数据仍然缺失。最值得注意的是，研究人员在4月份该人首次感染后没有检测抗体，这是以喉咙痛、咳嗽、头痛、恶心和腹泻为特征的。因此，尽管到目前为止的数据表明，大多数人在感染SARS-CoV-2后会产生有助于抵御SARS-CoV-2的抗体，但研究人员并不知道这名患者是否做到了这一点。然而，他们确实注意到，患者没有免疫功能受损，似乎也没有出现会使再次感染的可能性更大的情况。
在香港男子再次感染的第一份报告中，研究人员在患者首次感染后一度检测了抗体，但他们没有发现任何抗体。虽然这暗示这个人可能没有产生抗体(这可以解释再次感染的原因)，但研究人员说，他们需要在不同的时间点进行更多的测试，才能确定在第一次感染后缺乏抗体。重要的是，香港患者的第二轮新冠肺炎没有症状，这表明他确实有一些保护性免疫反应。
内华达州的病人就没那么幸运了。在5月下旬开始的第二次感染中，患者出现呼吸急促和咳嗽。后来的肺部扫描显示出非典型肺炎的迹象。该人住院治疗，需要持续的氧气支持。6月初，患者的SARS-CoV-2抗体检测呈阳性，但研究人员没有仔细检查抗体的类型。
总而言之，这起案件和第一起案件一样，留下了所有重大问题的悬而未决。正如耶鲁大学免疫学专家岩崎明子总结的那样：“这个病例突出了进一步检查COVID19再次感染的结果范围的必要性。随着越来越多的再感染病例将被报道，我们应该更好地了解自然感染后的免疫系统对疾病的防御能力有多好。“
note_en:
A 25-year-old resident of Reno, Nevada was infected with the pandemic coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, two times, about 48-days apart, with the second infection causing a more severe case of COVID-19 than the first and requiring hospitalization and oxygen support.
 That’s according to  a draft study, led by researchers at the University of Nevada and posted online. The study has not been published by a scientific journal and has not been peer-reviewed. Still, it drew quick attention from researchers, who have been examining data from the  first confirmed case of a SARS-CoV-2 reinfection, reported earlier this week.
 Reinfections with SARS-CoV-2 are not surprising—or even necessarily concerning. From person to person, immune responses to an infection develop along a spectrum, with some people mounting robust, protective responses and others being left with weaker responses. Amid the more than 24.5 million cases worldwide, it is completely expected to find some recovered patients who are not completely protected by their immune responses and are thus vulnerable to reinfection.
 The big question is: how common is this scenario? Researchers still do not know. This case may very well represent a very rare event, but there are many massive questions about SARS-CoV-2 immune responses that researchers are racing to understand. Why do some people develop more severe disease than others? For those who recover, what levels of immune responses will protect them from another infection? How many of the recovered will develop those protective responses? And how long do those protective responses last?
 While the new report of a reinfection is an important data point, it—like the reinfection report before it—does not answer any of these big questions.
  About all we can say from these reports is that reinfection is possible—at least for some individuals. Again, this isn’t surprising, but it is tricky to prove. Like the report earlier this week, the Nevada researchers turned to whole genome sequencing as evidence that their patient did indeed have two, distinct infections.
 Specifically, the researchers decoded the entirety of the SARS-CoV-2 genetic material collected from nasal swabs taken during the first and second illness. The researchers found that viruses present in the patient’s nose during the two illnesses had many small differences in their codes. The first virus had four code variations missing from the second—and the second virus had seven variations missing from the first. While small genetic changes tend to naturally accumulate in viruses over time, the researchers calculated that if the two illnesses were actually caused by the same virus, the virus would have had to evolve more than 3.6-times faster in this one patient than has been previously documented. Moreover, four of the changes seen between the two viruses could only be explained by genetic “reversions,” and the odds of these occurring are “vanishingly remote,” the authors say.
 Still, aside from the genetic evidence suggesting two different infections, there’s always the question of whether there was some mix-up with the samples. That is, perhaps the patient  did not have the different strains of virus plucked from their nose; perhaps the swabs were simply mislabeled or mishandled in the lab. To try to head off this concern, the researchers did genetic forensic testing on the liquid in which the swabs were stored and intermediate sample preparations. The researchers found that all the human-derived material scraped up and associated with the nasal swab samples came from the same person.
  While the researchers make a persuasive case that their patient was in fact infected twice, critical data about the patient’s immune responses are missing. Most notably, the researchers did not test for antibodies after the person’s first infection in April, which was marked by sore throat, cough, headache, nausea, and diarrhea. So, although data so far suggests that most people develop antibodies that can help protect against SARS-CoV-2 after an infection, the researchers don’t know if this patient did or not. They do note, however, that the patient was not immunocompromised and did not appear to have a condition that would make reinfection seem more likely.
 In the first report of a reinfection in a Hong Kong man, researchers tested for antibodies at one point after the patient's first infection, but they didn't find any. Though this hints that the person may not have developed antibodies (which could explain the reinfection), the researchers say they would need more testing at different time points to definitely show a lack of antibodies after the first infection. Importantly, the Hong Kong patient's second bout of COVID-19 was asymptomatic, suggesting that he did have some protective immune responses.
 The Nevada patient wasn't so lucky. In the second infection that began in late May, the patient developed shortness of breath and a cough. Lung scans later showed signs of atypical pneumonia. The person was hospitalized and required ongoing oxygen support. In early June, the patient tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 antibodies, but the researchers did not closely examine the types of antibodies.
 In all, this case, like the first, leaves all the big questions unanswered. As Yale immunology expert  Akiko Iwasaki summarized: “This case highlights the need for further examination of the range of outcomes from reinfection by COVID19. As more and more reinfection cases will be reported, we should get a better sense of how well the immune system after natural infection protects against disease.”
title: 新冠肺炎又一次再感染，这次二次感染更严重
title_en: Another COVID-19 reinfection: This time second infection was more severe

根据天文学家的一份新报告，SpaceX和其他公司正在发射的宽带卫星将不可避免地对天文学家观测夜空的能力产生负面影响。除了完全不发射卫星之外，没有任何缓解战略可以完全消除卫星对天文观测的影响，但该报告就卫星运营商如何将干扰降至最低以及天文台如何适应这些变化提出了建议。
本周发布的这份报告的标题是“卫星星座对光学天文学的影响和缓解建议”。该报告源于最近举行的卫星星座1号(SATCON1)研讨会，该研讨会由美国国家科学基金会NOIRLab和美国天文学会(AAS)联合举办。SpaceX的工程师参加了在线研讨会，但这份报告是由SATCON1科学组织委员会的成员撰写的，代表了他们的共识观点。委员会成员来自NOIRLab、AAS、亚利桑那州的洛厄尔天文台和斯图尔德天文台、智利的鲁宾天文台、密歇根大学、加州大学戴维斯分校、史密斯学院和天文学大学研究协会(AURA)。
两端都需要改变：星座运算符和天文台。SpaceX公司已经证明，运营商可以通过卫星主体定位、遮阳和表面变暗来减少反射的阳光。联合努力获得关于单个卫星(或星历)预测位置的更高精度的公开数据，可以在卫星通过期间实现一些指向避免和中间曝光快门。随着星座卫星数量的增加，天文台将需要采取更动态的调度和观测管理，尽管即使是这些措施对许多科学计划也是无效的。
到目前为止，SpaceX已经发射了600多颗卫星，OneWeb已经发射了74颗卫星。两家公司都计划最终将数万颗卫星发射到低地球轨道，并为缺乏快速有线服务的地区提供宽带。亚马逊还计划发射数千颗卫星。由于它们的低地球轨道(LEO)，这些卫星将提供比传统卫星网络更低的延迟。
SpaceX首席执行官埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)在3月份表示，他相信我们不会对天文发现造成任何影响。他说，卫星在发射后立即就能看到，因为它们正在轻微地翻滚，本质上是在眨眼或眨眼，但当它们在轨道上时，情况就不会这样了。一旦卫星稳定并提升轨道，它们就应该&##。
但SATCON1的报告称，SpaceX开始发射宽带卫星已经一年多了，天文学家现在已经积累了足够多的星座卫星观测数据，比如SpaceX和OneWeb正在发射的那些卫星，并对它们可能产生的影响进行了计算机模拟，从而开始了解问题的严重性和复杂性。
报告称，如果部署了许多公司和许多政府提出的10万颗或更多LEOsats，任何缓解措施的组合都不能完全避免卫星轨迹对目前和计划中的地面光学近红外天文设施的科学计划的影响。天文学家刚刚开始了解这一学科受到的各种影响。天文摄影、业余天文学，以及人类对恒星和银河系的体验都已经受到影响。
报告的作者说，为了得出他们的结论，他们对具有代表性的LEOSAT星座进行了模拟。报告称，将对天文学的危害降至最低的建议是基于天文学家和SpaceX公司的工作和合作提出的，但这些建议是面向广大受众的，特别是针对整个卫星星座行业的。
note_en:
Broadband satellites being launched by SpaceX and other companies will inevitably have a negative impact on astronomers' ability to observe the night sky, according to a new report by astronomers. There are no mitigation strategies that can completely eliminate the satellites' impact on astronomical observations—other than not launching satellites at all—but the report includes recommendations for how satellite operators can minimize disruption and how observatories can adjust to the changes.
 The  report released this week is titled, "Impact of Satellite Constellations on Optical Astronomy and Recommendations Toward Mitigations." The report resulted from the recent Satellite Constellations 1 (SATCON1) workshop, which was organized jointly by the National Science Foundation's  NOIRLab and the American Astronomical Society (AAS). SpaceX engineers participated in the online workshop, but the report was written by members of the SATCON1 Scientific Organizing Committee and represents their consensus views. The  committee members hail from NOIRLab, AAS, the Lowell and Steward observatories in Arizona, the Rubin Observatory in Chile, the University of Michigan, UC-Davis, Smith College, and the Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy (AURA).
  Changes are required at both ends: constellation operators and observatories. SpaceX has shown that operators can reduce reflected sunlight through satellite body orientation, Sun shielding, and surface darkening. A joint effort to obtain higher-accuracy public data on predicted locations of individual satellites (or ephemerides) could enable some pointing avoidance and mid-exposure shuttering during satellite passage. Observatories will need to adopt more dynamic scheduling and observation management as the number of constellation satellites increases, though even these measures will be ineffective for many science programs.
 SpaceX has so far launched over 600 satellites and OneWeb has  launched 74. Both companies plan to eventually launch tens of thousands of satellites into low-Earth orbits and provide broadband to areas that lack fast wired service. Amazon is also planning to launch  thousands of satellites. Because of their low-Earth orbits (LEO), the satellites will provide lower latency than traditional satellite networks.
  SpaceX CEO Elon Musk  said in March that he is "confident that we will not cause any impact whatsoever in astronomical discoveries." He said the satellites are visible immediately after launching because "they're tumbling a little bit" and essentially "blink" or "reflect in ways that is not the case when they're on orbit." Once satellites stabilize and raise their orbits, they shouldn't cause problems for astronomers, Musk claimed.
 But it's been over a year since SpaceX began launching broadband satellites, and astronomers have now "accumulated enough observations of constellation satellites like those being launched by SpaceX and OneWeb and run computer simulations of their likely impact to begin to understand the magnitude and complexity of the problem," the SATCON1 report said.
  "If the 100,000 or more LEOsats proposed by many companies and many governments are deployed, no combination of mitigations can fully avoid the impacts of the satellite trails on the science programs of current and planned ground-based optical-NIR [near-infrared] astronomy facilities," the report said. "Astronomers are just beginning to understand the full range of impacts on the discipline. Astrophotography, amateur astronomy, and the human experience of the stars and the Milky Way are already affected."
 Report authors said they "performed simulations of representative LEOsat constellations" in order to reach their conclusions. Recommendations for minimizing harms to astronomy "are based on work by and collaboration between astronomers and SpaceX," but these are "intended for a broad audience, and especially the satellite constellation industry as a whole," the report said.
title: 天文学家称，SpaceX卫星对夜空的影响无法消除
title_en: SpaceX satellites’ effect on night sky can’t be eliminated, astronomers say

20年前，这位企业家是中国南方一家电台的主持人。他主持了一个深夜节目，听众可以打电话进来，随心所欲地谈论任何事情，经常重复情感、关系或其他亲密的话题。那些无法接通电话线的人发送短信，赖随后会在广播中阅读。当时，对于广播电台来说，这是一种流行而有前途的模式，它与网络运营商分享从消息传递费用中赚取的收入。
现在，赖管理着中国最大的播客公司之一。荔枝在中文中的意思是荔枝，这是一种来自他家乡广东的芳香热带水果。当他开始给我讲述丽芝的故事时，他从办公室的茶几上拿起了一个红壳水果。
“我从在电台工作的日子中了解到，互动是音响业务中最好的盈利模式。多年来，在中国，广播电台的主要收入来源就是这些短信，“赖声川回忆道，说话的节奏轻松而缓慢，这在中国竞争激烈的企业家世界中是不寻常的。
总部本身感觉更像一个巨大的、诱人的咖啡店，而不是纳斯达克上市公司的一个高度紧张的工作场所。在广州一个由仓库改造而成的低层办公室里，这个地方点缀着精心照料的盆景和玻璃会议室后面坐在豆袋上的员工。
赖声川开发了这款应用，既用于播客生产，也用于消费，同时捕捉了供需双方。截至6月份，每月有5600万人使用立志。其中600多万是创作者，上传到该平台的播客累计数量创下2.15亿的新高。
note_en:
Twenty years ago, the entrepreneur was a host at a radio station in southern China. He ran a late-night program where listeners could call in and chat about anything as they wished, often riffing on feelings, relationships or other intimate subjects. Those who couldn’t get through the phone line sent text messages that Lai would then read on air. At the time, it was a popular and promising model for radio stations, which divided the revenue earned from messaging fees with network carriers.
 Now, Lai manages one of China’s largest podcast companies.  Lizhi   means “lychee” in Chinese, the aromatic tropical fruit from his hometown in the southern province of Guangdong. He picked up one of the red-shell fruits from a tea table in his office as he began telling me Lizhi’s story.
 “I learned from my days working in radio that interaction is the best monetization model in the audio business. For years in China, the main revenue source for radio stations was these text messages,” Lai reminisced, speaking at a relaxed, slow pace that is uncharacteristic in China’s dog-eat-dog entrepreneurial world.
  The headquarters itself felt more like a giant, inviting coffee shop than a high-strung workplace of a  Nasdaq-listed firm. Tugged away in a low-rise warehouse-turned-office in Guangzhou, the place is dotted with well-tended bonsai and staff sitting on bean bags behind glass meeting rooms.
 Lai built the app for podcast production as well as consumption, capturing both the supply and demand sides.  As of June, 56 million people used Lizhi monthly. Over 6 million of them were creators, and the cumulative number of podcasts uploaded to the platform hit a new record high of 215 million.
title: 播客是社交的：中国的立志如何让音频互动
title_en: Podcast is social: How China’s Lizhi makes audio interactive

这份备忘录由通用汽车负责全球产品开发、采购和供应链的执行副总裁道格·帕克斯(Doug Parks)发出，他宣布克尔维特团队将从该汽车制造商的全球产品团队转移到肯·莫里斯(Ken Morris)领导的自动驾驶和电动汽车项目。根据备忘录，这一转变将于9月1日生效。Inside EVS最先报道了这一变化。
“通用汽车致力于实现全电动的未来。我很高兴能让重新定义超级跑车性能、设计和可达性的团队担任关键角色，帮助我们将电动汽车整合并执行到同样的高标准，“莫里斯在一份电子邮件声明中说。
在备忘录中，帕克斯表示，此举将“帮助这个本已充满活力的团队进一步突破我们未来的电动汽车电池系统和组件在为客户带来兴奋和令人兴奋的性能方面所能提供的极限。克尔维特团队熟悉如何取悦客户和评论家，他们推出的中置引擎克尔维特赢得了世界的赞誉，并成为汽车史上获奖最多的汽车之一。“。
这一变化不会扰乱整个克尔维特车队。Tadge Juechter将继续担任Global Corvette的执行总工程师，并将在新的变种投放市场时继续领导团队。克尔维特的总工程师Ed Piatek现在将担任“未来产品”的总工程师，并将继续向Tadge汇报工作。根据备忘录，在这一新角色下，Piatek将在整个组织内合作开发未来的电动汽车项目。乔希·霍尔德(Josh Holder)一直担任克尔维特公司的项目工程经理，他将接替皮亚特克晋升为全球克尔维特公司的总工程师。
在这一组织变革之前，通用汽车在电动汽车和自动汽车技术方面进行了一系列宣布和投资。今年1月，这家汽车制造商表示，将向其底特律-哈姆特拉姆克(Detroit-Hamtramck)组装厂投资22亿美元，以生产全电动卡车和SUV，以及其子公司Cruise推出的一款自动驾驶汽车。通用汽车将在供应商工具和其他与推出新电动卡车相关的项目上额外投资8亿美元。
通用汽车将以一款全电动皮卡拉开这一新计划的序幕，这款皮卡将于2021年底投产。专为拼车而设计的电动自动驾驶穿梭车Cruise Origin将是底特律地区工厂投入生产的第二款汽车。上个月，通用汽车表示，到2023年将交付20辆电动汽车，其中大部分将使用该公司名为Ultium的新模块化电动汽车架构。
通用汽车已经在建设一座占地近300万平方英尺的工厂，该工厂将批量生产锂电池和电池组，这是该公司在未来三年将这些电动汽车推向市场的战略的基石。位于俄亥俄州洛德斯敦的Ultium Cells LLC电池制造厂是通用汽车和LG化学去年12月宣布的合资企业的一部分。
note_en:
The memo, sent by Doug Parks, GM’s executive vice president of global product development, purchasing and supply chain, announced that the Corvette team would move from the automaker’s global product team to the autonomous and electric vehicles program that is led by Ken Morris. The shift will go into effect September 1, according to the memo. The change was first  reported by InsideEVs.
 “General Motors is committed to an all-electric future. I’m excited to be putting the team that redefined supercar performance, design and attainability in key roles to help us integrate and execute our EVs to those same high standards,” Morris said in an emailed statement.
  In the memo, Parks said the move will “help this already dynamic team further push the boundaries on what our future EV battery systems and components can deliver when it comes to excitement and thrilling performance for our customers. The Corvette team is familiar with delighting customers and critics alike, having launched the mid-engine Corvette to world acclaim and becoming one of the most awarded cars in automotive history.”
 The change won’t disrupt the entire Corvette team. Tadge Juechter will remain executive chief engineer for Global Corvette and will continue to lead the team as new variants hit the market. Corvette’s chief engineer Ed Piatek will now be chief engineer of “future product” and will continue to report to Tadge. Under this new role, Piatek will work across the organization on future EV programs, according to the memo. Josh Holder, who has been Corvette’s program engineering manager, will be promoted to chief engineer for Global Corvette, replacing Piatek.
 The organizational change follows a series of announcements and investments from GM into electric vehicles and automated vehicle technology. In January, the automaker said it would invest  $2.2 billion into its Detroit-Hamtramck assembly plant to produce all-electric trucks and SUVs, as well as a self-driving vehicle unveiled by its subsidiary Cruise. GM will invest an additional $800 million in supplier tooling and other projects related to the launch of the new electric trucks.
 GM will kick off this new program with an all-electric pickup truck that will go into production in late 2021. The Cruise Origin, the electric self-driving shuttle designed for ridesharing, will be the second vehicle to go into production at the Detroit area plant. Last month, GM said it was on track to deliver 20 electric vehicles by 2023, most of which will use the company’s  new modular EV architecture, called Ultium.
 GM is already building a  nearly 3-million-square-foot factory that will  mass produce Ultium battery cells and packs, the cornerstone of the company’s strategy to bring those electric vehicles to market in the next three years. The Ultium Cells LLC battery cell manufacturing facility in Lordstown, Ohio is part of a joint venture between GM and LG Chem that was announced in December.

note:
在周五的一篇博客文章中，美国国家航空航天局(NASA)表示，它和合作伙伴波音(Boeing)的目标是不早于2020年12月重新进行无人驾驶测试。这将包括在没有任何人的情况下驾驶完全可重复使用的星际客机CST-100，对与机组人员的发射过程进行实时、全自动化的模拟，包括与国际空间站在轨道上的交会和对接，以及返程以及受控着陆和太空舱回收。
在去年12月的最初OFT期间，航天器按计划从佛罗里达州的卡纳维拉尔角起飞，搭载着联合发射联盟(ULA)的Atlas V，但在脱离运载火箭后不久就遇到了机上任务定时器的问题。这导致它的推进器熄火并消耗燃料，一个通信错误意味着NASA无法纠正这个问题，直到它使用了太多的燃料，才允许它按计划继续前往空间站。然而，太空舱确实安全返回地球，并在途中提供了有价值的测试数据。
NASA和波音随后对波音的软件开发计划以及该机构自己围绕公私合作的做法进行了全面审查，并确定了一些纠正行动。那次审查于7月份结束，合作伙伴现在一直在努力重返第二次演示飞行。
波音公司对这一重做有很大的依赖，因为NASA在商业机组计划中的另一个合作伙伴SpaceX现在其资格认证计划至少提前了一年。SpaceX公司最近成功完成了它的龙飞船的第一次载人演示任务，最早可能在10月份向国际空间站发射它的第一次运行的宇航员任务。
如果OFT-2像波音计划的那样进行，Starliner最早可能在2021年6月运送第一批乘客进行载人演示发射，目前计划在2021年12月执行第一次运营任务。当然，所有这些日期都可能会改变。
note_en:
In a  blog post on Friday, NASA said that it and partner Boeing were aiming to fly the re-do of that uncrewed test no earlier than December 2020. This will involve flying the fully reusable Starliner CST-100 without anyone on board, in a live, fully automated simulation of how a launch with crew would go, including a rendezvous and docking with the ISS on orbit, and a return trip and controlled landing and capsule recovery.
 During the original OFT last December, the spacecraft took off from Cape Canaveral in Florida atop a  United Launch Alliance   (ULA) Atlas V as planned, but encountered an issue with its onboard mission timer shortly after disengaging from the launch vehicle. That caused it to misfire its thrusters and expend fuel, and a communication error meant that NASA was not able to correct the issue until it had used too much fuel to allow it to continue to the Space Station as planned. The capsule did safely return to Earth, however, and provided valuable test data on the way.
  NASA and Boeing subsequently undertook a comprehensive review of Boeing’s software development program, as well as the agency’s own practices surrounding the public-private partnership, and determined a number of corrective actions. That review ended in July, and the partners have now been working to get back to a second demonstration flight.
 Boeing has a lot riding on this re-do, since NASA’s other partner in the Commercial Crew program,  SpaceX,   is now at least a year ahead in terms of its qualification program. SpaceX recently successfully completed its first crewed demonstration mission of its Dragon spacecraft, and could launch its first operational astronaut mission to the International Space Station as early as October.
 Provided OFT-2 goes as intended for Boeing, Starliner could be ferrying its first passengers for a crewed demonstration launch as early as June 2021, with plans for a first operational mission now set for December 2021. All these dates are subject to change, of course.
title: 波音和NASA计划12月第二次尝试无人轨道演示飞行
title_en: Boeing and NASA target December for second try at uncrewed orbital demonstration flight

note:
不和谐始于2015年，作为游戏玩家在游戏前、玩中和玩后相互交谈的一种方式。现在，这家聊天公司正在追求一个更广阔的愿景：成为你非工作生活中的松弛。
重要原因：在新冠肺炎的时代，人们比以往任何时候都更需要酒吧、餐馆和舞台等在线社交空间。
不和谐允许人们创建自己的在线社区空间，制定和执行规则，并决定是保持仅限邀请还是向公众开放。
用户可以在各种渠道分享消息，私下聊天，并进行小组讨论。最近，该公司增加了群组视频聊天功能。
不和谐将每个社区的空间称为服务器，但它不是由用户控制的单独计算机意义上的服务器。用户可以在不需要系统管理员知识的情况下运行服务器。
这种安排有利有弊。这意味着不和谐控制数据，并负责遵守执法。但这也意味着该服务可以执行自己的行为准则，处理信任、安全和安保问题。
他们的意思是：我们认为这在很大程度上是一种好处，首席执行官杰森·雪铁龙告诉Axios。它使我们能够确保不和谐是一个安全的谈话场所。
倒叙：Citron说，当该网站在2017年夏洛茨维尔集会后意识到白人至上主义者正在利用聊天平台进行组织时，不和谐迅速而公开地采取了行动。我们想明确指出，不和谐将是极端主义行为的敌对场所。
大局：雪铁龙表示，不和谐正试图启用可以提供不同功能的在线社交空间。
在现实世界中，礼堂看起来与咖啡厅的教室截然不同，每种设计都提供了空间如何使用的线索。
拥有超过1亿月度活跃用户的Discorde正试图在网上创建类似类型的空间。
在一定程度上，为了推动这一愿景的扩大，不一致性在6月份筹集了1亿美元，以Index Ventures为首，据报道，该公司的估值为35亿美元。
下一步是什么：雪铁龙的首要任务是让更多的游戏界以外的人了解不和谐。
他说，这是一种新的交谈和消磨时间的方式。如果你不真正投入其中，你可能会错过这项服务的魔力。
note_en:
Discord began in 2015 as a way for gamers to talk to one another before, during, and after play. Now, the chat company is pursuing a far broader vision: to be the Slack for your non-work life.
 Why it matters: In the age of COVID-19, more than ever before, people need the online equivalent of social spaces like bars, restaurants and stages.
  Discord allows people to create their own online community space, to set and enforce rules and decide whether to remain invite-only or open it to the public.
 Users can share messages in various channels, chat privately and have group discussions. More recently, the company has added group video chat.
 Discord calls each community's space a "server," but it's not a server in the sense of a separate computer controlled by the user. Users can run servers without needing system-administrator knowhow.
 This arrangement has pros and cons. It means Discord controls the data and is responsible for complying with law enforcement. But it also means the service can enforce its own code of conduct, handling trust, safety and security.
 What they're saying: "We view it as mostly a benefit," CEO Jason Citron told Axios . "It gives us the ability to ensure that Discord is a safe place to talk."
 Flashback: When the site realized after  the 2017 Charlottesville rally that white supremacists were using the chat platform to organize, Discord acted quickly and publicly, Citron said. "We wanted to make clear Discord would be a hostile place for extremist behavior."
 The big picture: Citron said Discord is trying to enable online social spaces that can serve different functions.
 In the real world, an auditorium looks quite different from a classroom from a coffee shop, with each design offering cues to how the space is used.
 Discord, which has more than 100 million monthly active users, is trying to create similar types of spaces online.
 In part to fuel that expanded vision, Discord raised $100 million in June, led by Index Ventures,  valuing the company at a reported $3.5 billion.
 What's next: At the top of Citron's list is getting more people outside the gaming world to know about Discord.
 "It is a new way to talk and spend time," he said. "If you don't really dive into it you can miss the magic of the service."
title: 不和谐看起来超出了游戏的根源
title_en: Discord Looks To Grow Beyond Its Gaming Roots

note:
Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格周四在一次全公司会议上抨击苹果，称这家iPhone制造商的应用商店是垄断的，对顾客有害。
扎克伯格通过网络直播对50,000多名员工说：“(苹果)作为手机上内容的看门人，拥有这种独特的控制力。”他补充说，这家总部位于加州库比蒂诺的公司的应用商店“阻止创新，阻止竞争”，并“允许苹果收取垄断租金”。
当这位Facebook首席执行官专门回答有关苹果屏蔽游戏相关应用程序的问题时，他发表上述言论之际，当局正在仔细审查这两家硅谷巨头的反垄断行为。上个月，扎克伯格和苹果首席执行官蒂姆·库克以及亚马逊和谷歌的负责人都在众议院听证会上作证，听证会审查了美国最大科技公司潜在的垄断行为。
扎克伯格的评论是另一个信号，表明这家社交网络的领导者和这家价值2万亿美元的电子设备制造商之间长期存在争议的关系中并没有失去爱。
扎克伯格周四表示：“这是可以真正改善人们生活的创新。”“而苹果只是对此犹豫不决。”
Facebook发言人拒绝对扎克伯格的言论置评。苹果发言人拒绝置评。
周四，苹果拒绝允许Facebook通知人们，这家iPhone制造商将收取通过Facebook应用程序的一项新功能进行的应用内购买的30%，该功能允许企业在该平台上销售在线活动的门票。在苹果告诉Facebook通知是“无关紧要的信息”之后，在苹果允许该功能通过之前，这家社交网络被迫将这条信息从其应用程序中删除。
本月早些时候，苹果还让Facebook从Facebook Gaming中去掉了一项名为Instant Games的功能，这是一款主要让人们观看其他人玩视频游戏的应用程序。
扎克伯格还批评了苹果即将推出的iPhone和iPad iOS 14操作系统的改变，这将使Facebook等公司更难通过广告瞄准使用这些设备的人。扎克伯格估计，一旦iOS 14发布，Facebook在iPhone和iPad上的定向广告将失去约50%的效力，并暗示如果Facebook可以在App Store之外分发其应用程序，就可以避免这种情况。
苹果本月成为首家估值达到2万亿美元的美国公司，其App Store的运营方式正受到全球的密切关注，App Store是在iPhone和iPad上购买和下载应用程序的唯一方式。在过去的几个月里，应用程序开发商批评该公司对每一次应用内购买收取30%的佣金，并且不允许iPhone和iPad用户从应用程序商店之外的设备上下载应用程序。
本月早些时候，苹果将热门视频游戏堡垒之夜踢出了应用商店，此前该游戏的创建者EpicePic添加了一项功能，允许玩家用自己的信用卡购买虚拟货币，绕过了苹果的30%提成。
作为回应，Etic对该公司提起诉讼，苹果威胁要撤销该公司的开发者账户。这一举动的影响超出了堡垒之夜的范畴，危及所有使用虚幻引擎的软件，虚幻引擎是史诗公司所有的平台，其他开发者用来制作视频游戏。
本周早些时候，加利福尼亚州的一名法官阻止苹果撤销Etic的开发者账户。
扎克伯格在对员工的讲话中表示，苹果封锁虚幻引擎的决定“只是一个极端激进的举动”，“相当有问题”。
扎克伯格还提到了《华尔街日报》(The Wall Street Journal)最近的一篇报道，该报道称，去年10月，扎克伯格在白宫与唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统共进私人晚宴时，提出了对中国互联网公司威胁美国企业的担忧。
不久之后，美国政府对字节跳动旗下的TikTok发起了国家安全审查，TikTok是在美国使用的最受欢迎的中国应用，也是Facebook最大的竞争对手之一。上个月，特朗普宣布TikTok进入“国家紧急状态”，并签署了一项行政命令，威胁要禁止该应用程序，除非将其出售给一家美国公司。周一，TikTok起诉特朗普政府。
扎克伯格周四告诉他的员工：“特朗普当然提到了中国或他正在采取的一些行动，但我不认为TikTok或任何这些东西都特别提到了那里。”
在本月早些时候的一次全体会议上，扎克伯格承认围绕TikTok存在“合理的”国家安全问题，但坚称禁止TikTok将开创“非常糟糕的长期先例”，“无论解决方案是什么，都需要极其谨慎和严肃地处理”。他在周四的讲话中重申了这些观点。
note_en:
Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg took a swing at Apple on Thursday, calling the iPhone maker's app store monopolistic and harmful to customers during a companywide meeting.
 “[Apple has] this unique stranglehold as a gatekeeper on what gets on phones,” Zuckerberg said to more than 50,000 employees via webcast. He added that the Cupertino, California–based company’s app store “blocks innovation, blocks competition” and “allows Apple to charge monopoly rents.”
 While the Facebook CEO was specifically answering a question about Apple blocking gaming-related apps, his comments came at a time where authorities are scrutinizing both Silicon Valley giants for antitrust behavior. Last month both Zuckerberg and Apple CEO Tim Cook, as well as the heads of Amazon and Google,  testified in a House of Representatives hearing examining potentially monopolistic practices at the United States' largest technology firms.
 Zuckerberg’s comments were another signal that there’s no love lost in the  long-contentious relationship between the leader of the social network and the $2 trillion electronic device maker.
 “That’s innovation that could really improve people’s lives,” Zuckerberg said on Thursday. “And Apple’s just balking at it.”
 A Facebook spokesperson declined to comment on Zuckerberg’s remarks. An Apple spokesperson declined to comment.
  On Thursday,  Apple refused to allow Facebook to notify people that the iPhone maker would collect 30% of in-app purchases made through a new feature on the Facebook app that lets businesses sell tickets to online events on the platform. After Apple told Facebook that a notification was “irrelevant information,” the social network was forced to scrub the message out of its app before Apple let the feature through.
 Earlier this month, Apple also  made Facebook strip a feature called Instant Games from Facebook Gaming, an app that primarily lets people watch other people playing video games.
 Zuckerberg also criticized a change in Apple’s upcoming iOS 14 operating system for iPhones and iPads that would make it  much harder for companies like Facebook to target people using those devices with ads. Zuckerberg estimated that Facebook’s ad targeting on iPhones and iPads would lose about 50% of its effectiveness once iOS 14 was released and suggested that if Facebook could distribute its apps outside the App Store, it could avoid that kind of scenario.
 Apple, which this month  became the first US company to be valued at $2 trillion, is under  global  scrutiny for the way it runs the App Store, the only way to buy and download apps on iPhones and iPads. In the last few months, app developers  criticized the company for taking a 30% commission on every in-app purchase, and not allowing iPhone and iPad users to download apps from outside the App Store on their devices.
 Earlier this month, Apple  kicked the popular video game  Fortnite off the App Store after Epic, the game’s creator, added a feature that let players buy virtual currency with their own credit cards, bypassing Apple’s 30% cut.
 In response, Epic filed a lawsuit against the company, and Apple threatened to revoke the company’s developer accounts. This move had implications beyond  Fortnite, jeopardizing all software that used the Unreal Engine, an Epic-owned platform used by other developers to make video games.
 Earlier this week, a California judge  stopped Apple from revoking Epic’s developer accounts.
 In his address to employees, Zuckerberg said Apple’s decision to block the Unreal Engine was “just an extremely aggressive move” that was “quite problematic.”
  Zuckerberg also addressed a  recent story in the Wall Street Journal, which reported that he had raised concerns about Chinese internet companies threatening American businesses in a private dinner with President Donald Trump at the White House in October.
 Shortly after, the US government launched a national security review into Bytedance-owned TikTok, the most popular Chinese app used in the United States, and one of Facebook’s largest competitors. Last month, Trump  declared TikTok a “national emergency” and signed an executive order that threatened to ban the app unless it was sold to an American company. On Monday, TikTok  sued the Trump administration.
 “Trump certainly brought up China or some of the actions that he was taking, but I don’t think TikTok or any of this stuff specifically came up there,” Zuckerberg told his employees on Thursday.
 At an all-hands meeting earlier this month, Zuckerberg conceded that there were “valid” national security questions around TikTok, but maintained that banning it would set a “ really bad long-term precedent,” and that “it needs to be handled with the utmost care and gravity whatever the solution is.” He repeated those views in his talk on Thursday.
title: 马克·扎克伯格说，苹果对iPhone的“垄断”是在收取“垄断租金”
title_en: Mark Zuckerberg Said Apple Is Charging 'Monopoly Rents' With Its 'Stranglehold' On iPhones

note:
2019年7月，阿拉皮亚·佐亚布(Alaphia Zoyab)与Facebook在印度的员工进行了视频通话，讨论了该国用户发布的约180条帖子，她工作的监督组织Avaaz称这些帖子违反了Facebook的仇恨言论规定。但Zoyab说，在长达一小时的会议进行到一半时，参加电话会议的Facebook最高级别官员Shivnath Thukral站起来走出了房间，说他还有其他重要的事情要做。
其中一个帖子是Shiladitya Dev，他是阿萨姆邦总理纳伦德拉·莫迪(Narendra Modi)的印度教民族主义政党印度人民党(BJP)的议员。他分享了一篇关于一名女孩据称被一名穆斯林男子下药和强奸的新闻报道，并补充了自己的评论：“这就是孟加拉穆斯林在2019年针对我们(原住民)的方式。”但Facebook没有将其删除，而是在会议结束后允许该帖子在网上保留了一年多，直到8月21日《时代》杂志联系Facebook询问此事。Facebook在给时代周刊的一份声明中说：“当Avaaz第一次向我们指出这一点时，我们对此进行了调查，我们的记录显示，我们将其评估为仇恨言论侵犯。”“我们在最初的审查中没有删除，这是我们的一个错误。”
Thukral当时是Facebook的印度和南亚公共政策总监。前雇员告诉时代周刊，他的部分工作是游说印度政府，但他也参与了有关当政客的帖子被主持人标记为仇恨言论时该如何行动的讨论。Facebook承认Thukral离开了会议，但他说他从来没有打算整个会议都留下来，加入只是为了把他在过去工作中认识的Zoyab介绍给他的团队。“Shivnath没有离开，因为这些问题并不重要，”Facebook在声明中表示，并指出该公司对会议期间提交的180个帖子中的70个采取了行动。
这家社交媒体巨头在被告是莫迪执政党成员的情况下，如何执行其仇恨言论政策，正受到越来越多的关注。活动人士表示，Facebook的一些政策官员与人民党关系过于密切，并指责该公司将其与政府的关系置于其宣称的使命之前，即从其平台上删除仇恨言论-特别是在执政党政客参与的情况下。例如，根据时代周刊看到的文件，Thukral曾与该党领导层合作，协助印度人民党2014年的竞选活动。
Facebook印度董事总经理阿吉特·莫汉(Ajit Mohan)在8月21日的一篇题为“我们是公开、透明和无党派的”的博客文章中否认了有关该公司对印度人民党表现出偏见的说法。他写道：“尽管我们的员工来自不同的政治派别和背景，但他们履行各自的职责，以公平和无党派的方式解读我们的政策。围绕内容升级的决定不是由一个人单方面做出的；相反，它们包含了公司内部不同团队和学科的观点。
8月14日，“华尔街日报”(The Wall Street Journal)援引Facebook现任和前任员工的话报道，Facebook在印度的最高政策官员安希·达斯(Ankhi Das)回击了其他Facebook员工，这些员工希望给一名印度人民党政客贴上“危险个人”的标签，并在他呼吁枪杀穆斯林移民后禁止他进入该平台。之后，Facebook发表了这篇博客文章。据“华尔街日报”报道，达斯辩称，惩罚邦议员T·拉贾·辛格(T.Raja Singh)会损害Facebook在印度的业务前景。(Facebook表示，Das的干预并不是辛格未被禁止的唯一原因，该公司仍在决定是否有必要禁止。)。
这些商业前景是相当大的。印度是Facebook最大的市场，有3.28亿人使用这个社交媒体平台。大约4亿印度人也在使用Facebook的即时通讯服务WhatsApp-在该国约5.03亿互联网用户中，这是一个相当大的比例。这些平台在印度政治中变得越来越重要；2014年选举后，Das发表了一篇评论文章，认为莫迪之所以获胜，是因为他在竞选中利用Facebook的方式。
但Facebook和WhatsApp也被用来传播仇恨言论和错误信息，尽管该公司正在努力打击，但这些信息被指责在印度各地社区紧张局势不断加剧的情况下，帮助煽动了针对少数群体的致命袭击。今年2月，印度人民党政治家卡皮尔·米什拉(Kapil Mishra)的一段演讲视频被上传到Facebook上，他在视频中告诉警方，除非他们驱逐占据德里一条道路的大部分穆斯林抗议者，否则他的支持者将自己动手。几个小时内就爆发了暴力骚乱。(在该案中，Facebook认为该视频违反了其关于煽动暴力的规定，并将其删除。)
在印度，WhatsApp也被带着致命的意图使用-例如，牛义务警员、袭击穆斯林的印度教暴徒和被控杀害牛的达利特人，牛在印度教中是一种神圣的动物。根据人权观察(Human Rights Watch)的数据，2015年5月至2018年12月期间，至少有44人被奶牛义务警员杀害，其中大多数是穆斯林。许多奶牛义务警员谋杀案发生在WhatsApp上谣言传播之后，私刑和殴打的视频也经常通过该应用程序分享。
“时代”周刊获悉，Facebook为了评估其在散布仇恨言论和煽动暴力方面的作用，委托撰写了一份关于其对印度人权影响的独立报告。此前未被报道的印度审计工作，在《华尔街日报》发表报道之前就开始了。据三名知情人士透露，这项调查由美国律师事务所Foley Hoag进行，将包括对Facebook高级员工和印度公民社会成员的采访。时代周刊看到了一封电子邮件。(2018年发布的一份关于缅甸的类似报告详细描述了Facebook在仇恨言论方面的失败，这些言论导致了前一年的罗辛亚种族灭绝。)。Facebook拒绝证实该报道。
但活动人士多年来一直在监控和报道印度教民族主义者的仇恨言论，他们告诉时代周刊，他们认为Facebook一直不愿监管印度人民党成员和支持者的帖子，因为它不想与控制着其最大市场的政府挑起冲突。分析人士和前员工表示，公司的结构方式加剧了问题，因为负责管理与政府关系的人也在决定是否应该惩罚发表仇恨言论的政客。
Facebook前首席安全官亚历克斯·斯塔莫斯(Alex Stamos)在5月份的推特上写道：“Facebook的一个核心问题是，一个政策组织既要负责平台的规则，又要让政府满意。”“地方政策负责人一般来自执政党，很少来自弱势族群、宗教信仰或种姓。这自然会使决策向权势集团倾斜。“。
一些积极分子对Facebook印度政策团队感到非常失望，以至于他们开始在报道仇恨言论时完全绕过它。在苏克拉尔离开后，Avaaz决定开始直接向Facebook位于加利福尼亚州门洛帕克的公司总部报告仇恨言论。“我们发现Facebook印度公司的态度完全是轻率、冷漠和不感兴趣的，”Zoyab说，他后来离开了Avaaz。另一个经常在印度Facebook上报告针对少数族裔的仇恨言论的组织表示，自2018年以来一直在这样做。出于对工作人员安全的担忧，该组织要求不具名。在一份声明中，Facebook承认，一些经常在印度标记仇恨言论的组织正在与Facebook总部联系，但表示这并没有改变判断帖子违反其规则的标准。
《华尔街日报》的爆料在印度掀起了一场政治丑闻，反对党政客呼吁正式调查Facebook涉嫌偏袒莫迪所在政党。这一消息也在公司内部引发了争执：据路透社报道，在一封内部公开信中，Facebook员工呼吁高管谴责“反穆斯林的偏见”，并采取更多措施，确保仇恨言论规则在整个平台上得到一致实施。这封信声称，印度政策团队中没有穆斯林员工；在回答《时代》杂志的问题时，Facebook表示，法律禁止其收集此类数据。
尽管企业聘请与政党有联系的游说者很常见，但活动人士表示，Facebook印度政策团队员工的历史，以及他们取悦政府的动机，在监管政客的仇恨言论方面造成了利益冲突。在加入Facebook之前，Thukral过去曾代表印度人民党工作。尽管如此，他还是参与了如何处理政客帖子的决策，这些帖子被主持人标记为在2019年选举期间违反了仇恨言论规则，这些前雇员告诉《时代》杂志(Time)。他在Facebook上的点赞包括一个名为“我支持纳伦德拉·莫迪”的页面。
前Facebook员工告诉时代周刊，他们认为苏克拉尔在2017年被聘用的一个关键原因是因为他被视为与执政党关系密切。2013年，在印度人民党最终成功地在2014年选举中赢得国家权力的竞选活动中，Thukral与该党高级官员合作，帮助运营了一个支持人民党的网站和Facebook页面。根据对相关人士的采访和时代周刊看到的文件，这个名为Mera Bharosa(印地语“我的信托”)的网站还举办了一些活动，其中包括一个旨在让学生报名投票的项目。一名志愿参加Mera Bharosa项目的学生告诉“时代”杂志，他不知道这是一场手术。
在给时代周刊的一份声明中，Facebook承认Thukral曾代表Mera Bharosa工作，但否认他过去的工作存在利益冲突，因为关于删除内容的重大决策涉及多个人。“我们知道，我们的一些员工过去曾在印度和世界其他地方支持过各种活动，”Facebook在向“时代”周刊(Time)发布的一份声明中表示，该声明回应了一系列详细的问题。“我们的理解是，Shivnath当时的志愿活动侧重于印度内部的治理主题，与您提出的内容问题无关。”
现在，苏克拉尔有一份更大的工作。2020年3月，他从Facebook的工作中晋升为WhatsApp的印度公共政策总监。新德里的技术政策专家告诉“时代”杂志，在这个职位上，Thukral的主要职责之一是管理公司与莫迪政府的关系。这是一项至关重要的工作，因为Facebook正试图将这款即时通讯应用程序转变为数字支付处理器-这是一个潜在价值数十亿美元的有利可图的想法。
今年4月，Facebook宣布将支付57亿美元收购印度最大电信公司Reliance Jio 10%的股份，该公司由印度首富穆凯什·安巴尼(Mukesh Ambani)所有。在5月份与投资者的电话会议上，Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格热情洋溢地谈到了这个商机。“印度有这么多人通过WhatsApp参与，我们只是认为这将是一个巨大的机会，让我们为人们提供更好的商务体验，帮助那里的小企业和经济，并随着时间的推移自己打造一家真正的大企业，”他说。他谈到了将WhatsApp Pay与Jio在印度各地庞大的小企业网络联系起来的计划。“这就是为什么我认为我们在印度进行深度投资真的很有意义.”
但WhatsApp在印度作为支付应用的未来取决于国家支付监管机构的最终批准，这一批准仍在等待中。Facebook在印度扩张的希望此前曾在2016年被一家国家监管机构粉碎，当时该国电信监管机构表示，Facebook只向包括自己在内的一些网站提供免费互联网访问的计划违反了网络中立规则。苏克雷尔在新职位上的首要任务之一是确保类似的问题不会打击Facebook在WhatsApp Pay上的雄心壮志。
“没有一家在印度的外国公司想成为政府的坏账。”
虽然监管机构在技术上是独立的，但分析人士表示，Facebook与印度首富的新关系可能会让WhatsApp Pay获得批准变得容易得多。行业分析公司Counterpoint Research的副总裁尼尔·沙阿(Neil Shah)表示：“有了安巴尼的支持，Facebook现在会更容易获得批准。”分析人士说，来自政府本身的善意也很重要。“在印度，没有一家外国公司愿意成为政府的坏账，”“亿万富翁拉杰”(The亿万富翁Raj)一书的作者詹姆斯·克拉布特里(James Crabtree)说。“Facebook非常希望与印度政府保持良好关系，在做会激怒他们的事情时，很可能会三思而后行。”
印度政府以前就表明过，它不怕粉碎外国科技公司的梦想。今年7月，在与中国发生地缘政治口角后，中国禁止了包括TikTok和微信在内的数十款中国应用。克拉布特里说：“印度正在悄悄走向一种数字保护主义。”“因此，Facebook背后的想法是，政府可能很容易转向总体上反对外国科技公司，特别是Facebook，特别是如果他们被视为针对主要政客的话。”
由于印度已经有数亿用户，还有数亿人还没有智能手机，但不久的将来可能会有，Facebook有动力避免这种可能性。耶鲁大学法学院(Yale Law School)研究科技平台监管的常驻研究员钦马伊·阿伦(Chinmayi Arun)表示：“Facebook过去曾表示，允许在其平台上发表仇恨言论没有商业利益。”“从印度发生的事情可以明显看出，这并不完全正确。”
Facebook表示，它正在努力打击仇恨言论。“我们想明确表示，我们谴责任何形式的仇恨，”Facebook印度董事总经理莫汉(Mohan)在8月21日的博客文章中说。“当印度公众人物发布的内容违反我们的社区标准时，我们已经并将继续删除这些内容。”
但对Facebook来说，清除仇恨言论仍然是一个令人望而生畏的挑战。在6月份的一次员工会议上，扎克伯格强调了米什拉在德里骚乱之前2月份的演讲，但没有点名，认为这是一个应该被撤职的职位的明显例子。米什拉演讲的原始视频在上传后不久就被删除了。但这段视频的另一个版本，有超过5600的点击量和一长串支持的评论，在网上持续了六个月，直到8月份《时代》周刊将其标记给Facebook。
